coredata.dc:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of cysteine with a graphene quantum dots-gold nanoparticles based core-shell nanosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Hai, Xin
Lin, Xin
Chen, Xuwei
Wang, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: Reduced graphene quantum dots (r-GQDs) coated gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) core-shell structure is developed as a nanosensor for highly selective and sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine. The reduction of nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) by NaBH4 produces r-GQDs, which serve as reductant for facilitating the conversion of HAuCl4 to AuNPs. The abundant hydroxyl groups and amino groups of r-GQDs not only promote the reduction of Au (III) into Au (0), but also facilitate the effective coating of r-GQDs onto AuNPs. The obtained core-shell structured AuNPs@r-GQDs are well dispersed and exhibit an intensive surface plasma band at 525nm. After the adsorption of Ag+ on the surface of AuNPs@r-GQDs, cysteine as cross-linking agent induces the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs and leads to their color change, which allows the sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine and offers a detection limit low to 5.6nM. More importantly, AuNPs@r-GQDs nanosensor exhibits very high detection selectivity towards cysteine against glutathione (GSH), even at a GSH concentration of 10000-fold higher than that of cysteine. The practicability of this nanosensor is demonstrated by the discriminative determination of cysteine in human plasma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reduced graphene quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles
Nanosensor
Cysteine
Glutathione
Colorimetric detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cys with AuNPs@r-GQDs nanosensor
Principle for colorimetric detection of Cys
Discriminative determination of Cys in human plasma

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration for the detection of Cys with AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite as nanosensor.

(A) TEM images of r-GQDs; insert: size distributions of r-GQDs; (B) AFM images of r-GQDs; insert: height profile along the line from a to b; (C) TEM images of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite; insert: size distributions (1) and HRTEM images (2) of AuNPs@r-GQDs; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of r-GQDs (a), fresh AuNPs@r-GQDs (b) (red line) and AuNPs@r-GQDs after standing for one month (c) (blue line); insert: photograph of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

High-resolution C1s XPS spectra of (A) N-GQDs; (B) r-GQDs; (C) AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite; (D) High-resolution Au4f spectra of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite; (E) XRD patterns of r-GQDs (a) and AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite (b); (F) FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs (a), r-GQDs (b), AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite (c).

TEM images (A) and AFM images (B) of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite in the presence of Ag+ and Cys; TEM images (C) and AFM images (D) of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite in the presence of Ag+ and GSH; (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite alone (a), in the presence of Ag+ (b); in the presence of 2μM Cys (c), and in the presence of Ag+ and 2μM Cys (d).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs@r-GQDs treated with various amounts of Cys (0–5μM) after the addition of Ag+ (1nmol); (B) Plot of A630/A525 versus Cys concentration at optimal conditions; Linear calibration graphs between A630/A525 and Cys concentration within 10–100nM (C) and 100–5000nM (D).

(A) Absorbance ratio of A630/A525 in the solution containing AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, Ag+ (1nmol) and various foreign species (100μM) or Cys at 100nM and 5μM; (B) The effect of different concentration of GSH on the detection of Cys.
Comparison of the present nanosensor for the detection of Cys with other similar procedures.
Determination results of Cys content in human plasma (n=3, at 95% confidence level).

head.ce:title: Highly selective and sensitive detection of cysteine with a graphene quantum dots-gold nanoparticles based core-shell nanosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Analytical Sciences, Department of Chemistry, Northeastern University, Box 332, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reduced graphene quantum dots (r-GQDs) coated gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) core-shell structure is developed as a nanosensor for highly selective and sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine. The reduction of nitrogen doped GQDs (N-GQDs) by NaBH4 produces r-GQDs, which serve as reductant for facilitating the conversion of HAuCl4 to AuNPs. The abundant hydroxyl groups and amino groups of r-GQDs not only promote the reduction of Au (III) into Au (0), but also facilitate the effective coating of r-GQDs onto AuNPs. The obtained core-shell structured AuNPs@r-GQDs are well dispersed and exhibit an intensive surface plasma band at 525nm. After the adsorption of Ag+ on the surface of AuNPs@r-GQDs, cysteine as cross-linking agent induces the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs and leads to their color change, which allows the sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine and offers a detection limit low to 5.6nM. More importantly, AuNPs@r-GQDs nanosensor exhibits very high detection selectivity towards cysteine against glutathione (GSH), even at a GSH concentration of 10000-fold higher than that of cysteine. The practicability of this nanosensor is demonstrated by the discriminative determination of cysteine in human plasma.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (Cys) is a thiol-containing amino acid and exists widely in proteins, cells and biological tissues. As a kind of life-related biothiol, Cys involves heavily in various biological processes such as detoxification and promoting metabolism for the capability of combining with Ag+, Hg2+ or Cu2+ to form insoluble mercaptides. Cys concentration is usually in the range of micromolar to millimolar in human plasma, and excessive Cys would cause neuronal damages [1]. Therefore, accurate monitoring of Cys content under physiological conditions is of highly importance owning to its biological functions. Up to now, various methodologies have been developed for the detection of Cys, including liquid chromatography [2], electrochemical methods [3], colorimetry [4] and spectrofluorimetry [5]. Among these approaches, colorimetric methods are most extensively used due to the simplicity and rapidness. On account of intrinsic properties of unique surface plasma resonance [6], high extinction coefficient [7] and apparent color variation [8], gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have been employed as successful colorimetric probe for the detection of biothiols including Cys, homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH), based on the transformation of AuNPs from dispersed state to aggregative state or vice versa, which is induced by the specific interaction between SH and Au [9–11]. Considering that all the biothiols contain SH group, these AuNPs-based sensors share similar detection performance towards Cys, Hcy and GSH, therefore the discriminative detection of each biothiol species in the presence of others is still quite challenging.

Graphene has been serving as excellent two-dimensional template for in-situ growth or self-assembled immobilization of metal nanoparticles attributed to their huge surface area [12]. A graphene-AuNPs composite has been reported to possess both nature of graphene and gold nanoparticles, which plays a wide role in catalysis, electrochemistry and other fields [13–15]. Although the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of graphene provide active sites for the nucleation of AuNPs, assistant reductants such as hydrazine hydrate [12] and sodium citrate [16] are usually added to improve the reducibility of graphene. Recently, carbon nanoparticles [17–19] and graphene oxide NPs [20,21] are served as reductant or stabilizing agent for synthesis of AuNPs under facile conditions, providing an alternative to prepare AuNPs without extra assistant reductants. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanoscale disc-like graphene of less than 100nm in lateral dimension [22,23]. They own merits of low cytotoxicity, favorable biocompatibility and excellent optical properties such as anti-photobleaching, intensive photoluminescence and ultraviolet absorption due to the quantum confinement effect and edge effect [24]. Hence, GQDs have been widely employed as brilliant sensing platforms for the determination of metal ions and biomolecules [25–29], as well as the excellent probe for bio-imaging [30,31]. The abundant oxygen-containing groups on GQDs, e.g., carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, provide the feasibility for functional modification, and meanwhile offer GQDs favorable reducibility to act as electron acceptor and electron donor [19,32]. As the oxygen containing groups of GQDs can effectively prevent AuNPs from aggregation, the GQDs/AuNPs composite exhibits favorable dispensability and stability in aqueous medium, making it excellent oxidant for veratryl alcohol with selective oxidation capability [33].

Herein we report a one-pot chemical reduction of AuCl4 − for the preparation of AuNPs with r-GQDs as both reductant and stabilizer. N-GQDs are firstly prepared via a facile hydrothermal approach with a high productive yield, and then r-GQDs are obtained by reducing the N-GQDs with sodium borohydride. More hydroxyl groups are introduced onto r-GQDs via reduction process. Hydroxyl and amino groups endow r-GQDs excellent electron-donating capability and facilitate the formation and stabilization of AuNPs, resulting in core-shell structure of AuNPs@r-GQDs. Ag+ and cysteine induce the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, due to adsorption of Ag+ on the surface of nanocomposite and specific interactions between the adsorbed Ag+ and Cys. The aggregation leads to color change of AuNPs@r-GQDs, and thus a nanosensor is developed for the detection of cysteine. Notably, the presence of high concentration of GSH does not induce aggregation of the AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, due to the self-assembly of GSH on the surface of the nanocomposite. This offers the AuNPs@r-GQDs nanosensor very high selectivity for Cys against GSH.

Citric acid (CA), urea, sodium borohydride (NaBH4), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), boric acid (H3BO3), acetic acid (HAc) and methyl cyanide (MeCN) were purchased from Signopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cysteine (Cys) and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Aladdin Chem. Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Alanine (Ala), valine (Val), leucine (Leu), isoleucine (Ile), phenylalanine (Phe), tryptophan (Try), methionine (Met), proline (Pro), glycine (Gly), serine (Ser), threonine (Thr), tyrosine (Tyr), asparagines (Asn), histidine (His), lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg), aspartic acid (Asp), glutamic acid (Glu), were the products of Beijing Biotopped Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Glutathione (GSH) was provided by Biosharp (Hefei, China). Homocysteine (Hcy) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, USA). All the chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without further purification. Deionized water of 18MΩcm was used throughout.

UV-vis absorption spectra were acquired on a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) with a 1.0cm optical path. Photoluminescence spectra were measured on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High Technologies, Japan) providing a 0.5cm quartz cuvette. The excitation and emission slits were set as 5.0nm, along with a scan speed of 1200nmmin−1. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEM-2100F field-emission electron microscope operating at 200kV (JEOL, Japan). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images were obtained on a Bruker Dimension icon atomic force microscopy using ScanAsyst in air mode with a tip type of ScanAsyst-air (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on an ESCALAB 250 surface analysis system (Thermo, USA) with an Al Kα 280eV excitation source. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) size were recorded on a Zetasizer Nano S90 potential and nano-particle size analyzer (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were received from a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany) with Cu-Ka irradiation (Cu Kα=1.54Å) in the range of 2θ from 10 to 90°. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were conducted by using a Nicolet-6700 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA) from 400 to 4000cm−1. Raman spectra were collected on an XploRA Raman spectrometer (Horiba Co., France) with excitation wavelength at 532nm.

Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were firstly prepared according to a previously reported procedure with some modifications [34]. Briefly, 0.21g citric acid and 0.18g urea were mixed with 5mL water and stirred to form a clear solution. Then the solution was transferred into a 25mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 160°C for 4h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was placed in dark for overnight. After rotary evaporation to remove ammonia, the obtained solution was dialyzed over deionized water in dialysis bag (MWCO membranes, 100∼500Da) for 24h to remove the residual reactant. Finally, N-GQDs powder was achieved via lyophilization and re-dispersed in deionized water.

To improve the content of hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-GQDs, sodium borohydride was introduced to achieve reduced nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (r-GQDs) [35]. Briefly, 0.01g NaBH4 was rapidly added into the solution of N-GQDs (1mgmL−1, 10mL) and stirred for 2h at room temperature. The obtained r-GQDs solution was dialyzed over deionized water in dialysis bag (MWCO membranes, 100–500Da) for 24h to remove the residual NaBH4. Finally, r-GQDs was achieved via lyophilization and re-dispersed in deionized water.

All glasses used in the following procedures were immersed in a fresh aqua regia (HCl: HNO3 =3: 1, V/V) overnight and rinsed thoroughly with deionized water. AuNPs were prepared directly via the reduction of HAuCl4 by using r-GQDs as reductant and stabilizer by following a reported procedure [36]. Firstly, 10mL of HAuCl4 solution (0.1mM) was heated to boiling, followed by rapid addition of 50μL r-GQDs solution (10mgmL−1) under vigorous stirring. The obtained purple-red solution was boiled for 10min, with further stirring for 30min. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 8000rpm for 10min, and the collected sediment was re-dispersed into water. Finally, AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite was filtered through a 0.1μm microporous membrane to remove the precipitate and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric detection of Cys with AuNPs@r-GQDs nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of AgNO3 (10.0mM) and Cys (10.0mM) were prepared in deionized water freshly, and working solutions were obtained by appropriate dilution. The colorimetric detection of Cys was achieved by following steps. Firstly, 10μL AgNO3 solution (100μM) was mixed with 100μL AuNPs@r-GQDs (A=0.75, at λ 525nm), followed by the addition of 90μL of Cys (0–5μM) and 20μL BR buffer (0.04M at pH 5). The mixture was then allowed to react for 10min at 37°C. The absorbance of the final solution at 525nm and 630nm were recorded with a 1.0cm optical path. The calibration curve was achieved by plotting Cys concentration versus the absorbance ratio of A630nm/A525nm.

During the preparation process of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, NaBH4 was employed for the reduction of N-GQDs to achieve r-GQDs with wealthy OH groups. The as-prepared r-GQDs were monodispersed uniform circular sheets, with an average diameter of 8nm in lateral size and average topographic height of 0.531nm (Fig. 1 A, B). Raman spectra of r-GQDs displays a typical D band (1370cm−1) corresponding to disordered sp3 defects and a G band (1560cm−1) relating to ordered sp2 CC bond with a ID/IG ratio of 0.72, suggesting that the r-GQDs are crystalline and graphitic. (Fig. S1). The improved fluorescence of r-GQDs over N-GQDs (Fig. S2) suggests that there are more OH groups on r-GQDs than N-GQDs, as OH groups contribute greatly to the blue emission [35]. The abundant OH on r-GQDs not only is able to reduce HAuCl4 from Au (III) into Au (0) for the generation of AuNPs, but also facilitates the effective coating of AuNPs with r-GQDs layer via hydrogen-bonding [37], giving rise to core-shell structured AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite of excellent stability. TEM image indicated that the size of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite is 18–21nm (Fig. 1C, insert 1), which was well consistent with the results of DLS analysis (Fig. S3). The interplanar spacing of the adjacent lattice fringe of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite was 0.241nm (Fig. 1C, insert 2), attributed to the (111) lattice space of metallic Au (JCPDS 04-0784) [33]. HRTEM images (Fig. S4A, B) clearly show the crystalline structure of inner AuNPs core and the outer carbon shell with a thickness of 1.2nm. In the fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns (insert of Fig. S4A), the diffuse ring corresponding to typical carbon at the centre, and the regular diffraction spots related to the Au are clearly observed. At the same time, the diffraction rings of crystalline Au for inner core was observed in the corresponding SAED patterns (Fig. S4C). Moreover, the elemental mappings (Fig. S5A–D) further demonstrated that Au and C are located in the core and shell region of the obtained nanocomposite, respectively. AuNPs usually possess unique optical properties in visible region because of the surface plasma oscillation of free electrons [38]. The solution of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite was purple-red and shown a typical surface plasma resonance (SPR) band at 525nm (Fig. 1D, line b) with a weak peak in 329nm related to r-GQDs. As for r-GQDs, no absorption peak was observed at 525nm, while an obvious peak located in 329nm, attributing to n-π* transition of CO (Fig. 1D, line a) [34]. The strong interaction between the amide groups and AuNPs makes r-GQDs as an excellent stabilizer, offering the formed AuNPs with favorable dispersion stability [39,40]. It was illustrated that AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite was extremely stable and no aggregation took place even after stocking for one month (Fig. 1D, line c).

XPS characterizations demonstrated the variation of chemical composition and structure from N-GQDs to r-GQDs and AuNPs@r-GQDs. In comparison with C1s XPS patterns of N-GQDs, the band intensity of COH at 286.1eV increased in r-GQDs (Fig. 2 A, B) [17], suggesting that the carbonyl groups (CO) in N-GQDs were reduced to hydroxyl groups (-OH) by NaBH4, resulting in the improved OH groups on the generated r-GQDs [35]. After the formation of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, the fraction of COH decreased from 0.40 in r-GQDs to 0.25 in AuNPs@r-GQDs (Fig. 2C, Table S1), due to the consumption of OH groups in the reduction of Au (III) into Au (0) [19]. The changes of OH groups were also well reflected by the O1s XPS patterns (Fig. S6, Table S2). The appearance of Au4f signature (Fig. 2D) indicated the existence of Au in the final AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, and the peaks of Au4f7/2 (83.2eV, 84.8eV, 86.9eV) and Au4f5/2 (88.3eV) were consistent with the Au0 state [41]. The high-resolution N1s XPS spectrum of N-GQDs, r-GQDs and AuNPs@r-GQDs displayed typical peaks of pyridinic N (398.6eV), pyrrolic N (399.5eV) and graphitic N (401eV) (Fig. S7) [34,42]. The total N content in GQDs, r-GQDs and AuNPs@r-GQDs was 5.09%, 2.38% and 0.69%, respectively. Zeta potential of r-GQDs was −27.5mV at pH 7.0, which was lower than that of N-GQDs (−17.9mV), but higher than that of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite (−22.6mV) (Fig. S8). This evidence indicated that there are more OH groups on r-GQDs than N-GQDs. XRD patterns of r-GQDs (Fig. 2E) exhibited a broad peak centered at about 22°, which was resulted from the highly disordered carbon atoms and confirmed the graphene structure of the obtained r-GQDs [43]. The AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite shown the characteristic Bragg’s reflections corresponding to cubic Au, along with the reflection of r-GQDs [44], and the diffraction peaks at 38.2°, 44.4°, 64.7°, 77.7° and 82.0° were assigned to the lattice plane of Au (111), Au (200), Au (220), Au (311) and Au (222), respectively, which were consistent with SAED patterns [13]. Fig. 2F illustrated the FT-IR spectra of N-GQDs, r-GQDs and AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite. The broad absorption bands at 3425cm−1 and 3209cm−1 of N-GQDs were assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH. The bands at 1707cm−1, 1583cm−1 and 1182cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO, CC and CO, respectively [45]. Besides, bands of typical stretching vibration modes of CN also appeared at 1404cm−1 in the spectrum of N-GQDs [46]. Compared to N-GQDs, an increase was encountered for the relative intensity of OH band at 3425cm−1 in r-GQDs, indicating the increase of OH groups in r-GQDs after the reduction of N-GQDs with NaBH4. The consumption of OH in the reduction of HAuCl4 led to the decrease of OH intensity in the spectrum of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite. The disappearance of signal at 3209cm−1 (NH) in r-GQDs and AuNPs@r-GQDs was attributed to the consumption of NH2 groups during the reduction of N-GQDs to r-GQDs and the formation of AuNPs@r-GQDs. Additionally, the band of CO at 1632cm−1 shifted to lower frequency in AuNPs@r-GQDs when compared with that in r-GQDs, due to the formation of hydrogen-bond. These reflections on the intensity of OH were in good agreement with that of XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle for colorimetric detection of Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrated the principle for the colorimetric detection of Cys. The AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite exhibited a typical SPR band at ca. 525nm. The strong binding of Ag+ onto the surface of r-GQDs has been well illustrated by the ‘hard and soft acids and bases’ theory (HSAB), where Ag+ is a soft acid and the nitrogen atoms (pyridinic N (C-N-C), pyrrolic N (C2-N-H) and graphitic N (NC3)) in r-GQDs surface serve as soft bases [47]. Ag+ exhibits a strong affinity with r-GQDs and forms conjugation of the Ag+-r-GQDs complex. The specific Ag-S bonds would lead to the formation of C-Ag+-C base pairs between Cys and Ag+ [48]. Except the specific Ag-S interaction, Cys molecules can also interact with the Ag+ via the interaction between Ag+ and the nitrogen of amino acids [49,50]. Thus, Cys acted as cross-linking agent for the AuNPs@r-GQDs pairs, thereby inducing the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocoposite (Fig. 3 A, B). As a result, color changes of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite were encountered with the increase of Cys concentration (Fig. S9). At the same time, the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite resulted in a new absorption centered at 630nm (Fig. 3E). The absorbance ratio of λ630nm/λ525nm could be readily used for the sensitive detection of Cys. There was hardly any variation observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite in the presence of either Cys or Ag+ alone. Fig. S10 illustrated the absorbance ratios (A630nm/A525nm) of AuNPs@r-GQDs in the absence and presence of Ag+ under different concentration of Cys (0–100μM). Nearly no change on A630nm/A525nm was observed when Ag+ was not existed in the system; even the Cys concentration was high up to 100μM. While with the presence of Ag+, clear variation of A630nm/A525nm could be observed even when Cys concentration was low to 10nM. This well suggests the synergistic effect of Ag+ on the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite.

The experimental parameters affecting the detection of Cys, e.g., Ag+ dosage, pH value, incubating temperature and time, were thoroughly investigated. The results were displayed in Fig. S11. At optimal experimental conditions, Cys induced the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite, leading to the decrease of absorbance at 525nm and the increase of absorbance at 630nm (Fig. 4 A). A novel nanosensor for colorimetric determination of Cys content was therefore proposed by elucidating the relationship between Cys concentration and the absorbance ratio of A630nm/A525nm. As illustrated in Fig. 4B, linear calibration ranges were obtained within 10–100nM and 100–5000nM, with regression equations of A630/A525 =3.52×10−3Ccys +0.209 and A630/A525 =1.03×10−4Ccys +0.524 (Fig. 4C, D). A limit of detection of 5.6nM Cys was achieved for the present nanosensor, with a precision of 0.7% RSD at a concentration level of 80nM (n=11).

For direct quantification of Cys in human plasma, the potential interfering effects of some species frequently encountered in biological samples were tested by analyzing AuNPs@r-GQDs in the presence of various amounts of coexisting species. It was evident that within a ±5% error range, 100μM of the coexisting amino acids and other species, e.g., methionine (Met), phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), isoleucine (Ile), threonine (Thr), alanine (Ala), lysine (Lys), valine (Val), arginine (Arg), proline (Pro), serine (Ser), histidine (His), leucine (Leu), glutamic acid (Glu), tryptophan (Try), asparagines (Asn), aspartic acid (Asp), tyrosine (Tyr), ascorbic acid (AA), glutathione (GSH), anions (Cl−, PO4 3−, CO3 2−), cations (Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+) and 10μM Hg2+ caused no interference to the detection of Cys (Fig. 5 A).

In comparison with some recently reported Cys sensing systems associated with AuNPs, the present nanosensor exhibits a very low limit of detection and high selectivity against GSH as shown in Table 1
[4,8,10,17,51–55]. Generally, Cys and GSH have similar mercapto structures and give rise to comparable response in AuNPs-based colorimetric/fluorescent sensing systems [56]. Thus for the selective detection of Cys, the interfering effect from GSH is among the most serious problem. In this respect, further investigations have been directed to the detection of Cys at 100nM and 5μM in the presence of various concentration of GSH (0–2.5mM). At a concentration of up to 1.0mM GSH, no interference was encountered for the detection of Cys (Fig. 5B). That is, selective detection of 100nM Cys could be achieved in the presence of 10000-fold GSH. Such a high selectivity for Cys against GSH might be contributed by the self-assembly of GSH onto the r-GQDs layer. r-GQDs are rich in hydroxyl groups, which provide a mass of hydrogen-bonding and electrostatic sites for GSH adsorption [57]. It is reported that GSH is stable at pH<4 and prone to convert into oxidized glutathione (GSSG) at higher pHs. The oxidized disulfide form GSSG and its derivatives can self-assemble into transparent and thermoreversible gels [58], which tend to encapsulate AuNPs preferentially [59]. In the present study, the reaction was performed at pH 5, at which r-GQDs in the external layer of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite served as a template for the immobilization of GSH, where GSH was oxidized in situ and self-assembles into transparent gels (Fig. 3C, D). Self-assembly of GSH protected the AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite from being aggregated and resulted in a longer inter-particle distance, which eventually contributed to the high selectivity of Cys against GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discriminative determination of Cys in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability of AuNPs@r-GQDs-based nanosensor, Cys contents in a series of human plasma samples were determined. Human plasma samples were provided by the Hospital of Northeastern University (Shenyang, China), which were de-proteinized by using MeCN in advance [59]. The determination was performed at the optimal conditions. 90μL of 100-fold diluted plasma was mixed with 100μL AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite (A=0.75, at λ 525nm) and 10μL of Ag+ solution (100μM), followed by the addition of 20μL of BR buffer (0.04M, at pH 5). The reaction mixture was incubated at 37°C for 10min, and afterwards the absorbance at 525nm and 630nm were recorded. The analytical results were summarized in Table 2 . In addition, spiking recoveries of 89.3-114.6% were achieved for Cys in the human plasma samples.

We have prepared a core-shell structure of AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite by exploring r-GQDs as both reductant and stabilizer for the AuNPs. AuNPs@r-GQDs nanocomposite is extremely stable in aqueous medium with an intensive surface plasma band at 525nm. As an excellent cross-linking agent, Cys could induce the aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs by the adsorption of Ag+ on its surface, due to the specific interaction between Ag+ and Cys. The aggregation of AuNPs@r-GQDs leads to their color change, and a novel sensing approach for Cys is thus developed, with a detection limit of 5.6nM. It is noticeable that the present nanosensing system offers very high selectivity to Cys against GSH. This is particularly useful for the sensing of Cys in biological sample matrixes, in the presence of high concentration of GSH.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21475017, 21235001, and 21275027), Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N150502001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.10.169.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective determination of cysteines through precolumn double-labeling and liquid chromatography followed by detection of intramolecular FRET
Selective electroanalytical assay for cysteine at a boron doped diamond electrode
A colorimetric sensor for determination of cysteine by carboxymethyl cellulose-functionalized gold nanoparticles
Sensitive detection of cysteine based on fluorescent silver clusters
One-phase synthesis of water-soluble gold nanoparticles with control over size and surface functionalities

Hg2+-mediated aggregation of gold nanoparticles for colorimetric screening of biothiols
Label-free colorimetric detection of biothiols utilizing SAM and unmodified Au nanoparticles
Influence of molecular assembly and NaCl concentration on gold nanoparticles for colorimetric detection of cysteine and glutathione
Colorimetric detection of thiol-containing amino acids using gold nanoparticles
Fabrication of hybrids based on graphene and metal nanoparticles by in situ and self-assembled methods
Photocatalytic degradation of dyes over graphene-gold nanocomposites under visible light irradiation
Graphene/AuNPs/chitosan nanocomposites film for glucose biosensing
A novel hydrogen peroxide biosensor based on Au-graphene-HRP-chitosan biocomposites
Surface modification of graphene nanosheets with gold nanoparticles: the role of oxygen moieties at graphene surface on gold nucleation and growth
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
Novel fluorescent matrix embedded carbon quantum dots for the production of stable gold and silver hydrosols
Hydroxyl-rich C-dots synthesized by a one-pot method and their application in the preparation of noble metal nanoparticles
Fabrication of gold nanoparticle/graphene oxide nanocomposites and their excellent catalytic performance

Green, low-cost synthesis of photoluminescent carbon dots by hydrothermal treatment of willow bark and their application as an effective photocatalyst for fabricating Au nanoparticles-reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites for glucose detection
Chaotic dirac billiard in graphene quantum dots
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
The influence of edge structure on the electronic properties of graphene quantum dots and nanoribbons
Synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive, label-free detection of Fe (III) in aqueous media
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
In situ growth of surfactant-free gold nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for electrochemical detection of hydrogen peroxide in biological environments
Carbon quantum dot-based nanoprobes for metal ion detection
Photochemical deposition of surface-clean silver nanoparticles on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for sensitive colorimetric detection of glutathione
Graphene quantum dots derived from carbon fibers
Target delivery and cell imaging using hyaluronic acid-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Photoinduced electron transfers with carbon dots
Selective oxidation of veratryl alcohol with composites of Au nanoparticles and graphene quantum dots as catalysts
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Investigating the surface state of graphene quantum dots
Preparation and characterization of Au colloid monolayers
Facile “green” synthesis, characterization, and catalytic function of β-D-glucose-stabilized Au nanocrystals
4-amino-6-hydroxy-2-mercaptopyrimidine capped gold nanoparticles as fluorophore for the ultrasensitive and selective determination of L-cysteine
Stability of the DMF-protected Au nanoclusters: photochemical, dispersion, and thermal properties
Nitrogen and sulfur codoped graphene quantum dots as a new fluorescent probe for Au3+ ions in aqueous media
Graphene uniformly decorated with gold nanodots: in situ synthesis, enhanced dispersibility and applications
Catalyst-free synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene via thermal annealing graphite oxide with melamine and its excellent electrocatalysis
Sulfur and nitrogen binary doped carbon dots derived from ammonium thiocyanate for selective probing doxycycline in living cells and multicolor cell imaging
Carbon nanodots as ligand exchange probes in Au@C-dot nanobeacons for fluorescent turn-on detection of biothiols
Fluorescent probes for “off-on” highly sensitive detection of Hg2+ and L-cysteine based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots
One-pot green synthesis of oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their potential application in pH-sensitive photoluminescence and detection of mercury(II) ions
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots: “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Ag+ ions
Highly sensitive and selective detection of thiol-containing biomolecules using DNA-templated silver deposition
Structural chemistry and geochemistry of silver-sulfur compounds: critical review
Part I: surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy investigation of amino acids and their homodipeptides adsorbed on colloidal silver
A DNA-gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric competition assay for the detection of cysteine
Simple and sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine based on ssDNA-stabilized gold nanoparticles
Spectrophotometric determination of glutathione and cysteine based on aggregation of colloidal gold nanoparticles
Selective optical sensing of biothiols with Ellman’s reagent: 5,5′-Dithio-bis(2-nitrobenzoic acid)-modified gold nanoparticles
Anti-aggregation of gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric sensor for glutathione with excellent selectivity and sensitivity
Selective detection of cysteine and glutathione using gold nanorods
Self-assembly of natural tripeptide glutathione triggered by graphene oxide
Self-assembly and gelation of oxidized glutathione in organic solvents
A dual-mode nanosensor based on carbon quantum dots and gold nanoparticles for discriminative detection of glutathione in human plasma

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xin Hai received her bachelor degree (2013) and Master degree (2015) in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University (China). She now is a Ph.D student at the Research Centre for Analytical Sciences in Northeastern University, China. Her work is devoted to the functionalization of graphene quantum dots and its applications in biological assays.

Xin Lin received Master degree(2016) in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University (China). She is currently a Ph.D student in the Department of Chemistry at Northeastern University. Her work is focus on the applications of sensor array based on graphene quantum dots.

Xu-wei Chen is a professor at the Research Centre for Analytical Sciences in Northeastern University, China. Prof. Chen received his PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry from Northeastern University(China) in July 2007. His current research interests focus on the development of new techniques and materials for the isolation of trace biomacromolecules from complex biological samples.

Jian-hua Wang is currently a professor at the Research Centre for Analytical Sciences in Northeastern University, China. Heobtained his B.Sc from the Department of Chemistry, Nankai University, and M.Sc from Jilin University, China. He was awarded a PhD degree by Technical University of Denmark in 2002. He has been serving as an Associate Editor for TALANTA since Dec. 2004. His current research interest is focused on the exploitation of flow analysis protocols and hyphenation techniques, isolation of trace level of analytes of biologically significant (metals and biomacromolecules) from complex sample matrices and life science analysis.
